title: Commuter loss and retention in US employment centers during COVID-19 and its aftermath
data_sources: MIT Election Lab U.S. House returns

data_scope.employment_center_count: not stated in abstract/data statement; defined by identification method and detailed in Supplementary Information
data_scope.non_recovering_share: approximately one-quarter of employment centers did not recover

core_conclusion: Using >20 million monthly device traces (2019-2024), the authors identify US employment centers (ECs) and build a time-dependent commuting network, then use ML to explain commuter loss and recovery. Central-city ECs lost more commuters than suburban ones but recovered faster; centers with workers from Democratic-leaning districts lost more commuters; and about one-quarter of ECs never recovered from the initial pandemic shock.
why_it_matters: Distinguishes a structural break from a temporary dip across a full five-year window; reframes resilience as depending on geography and workforce composition; operationalizes 'employment center' from real-time mobility data; and offers a reproducible template for assessing the localized effects of future exogenous shocks.
why_nature: Couples urban space to commuter behavior and to downtown/transit/equity policy; national-scale, mechanism-seeking evidence; a methodological platform (EC identification + time-dependent commuting networks from geolocation) with open code and aggregated data; and high topical timeliness.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatially-aware explainable ML (MGWR + SHAP) on EC resilience -> transfers to telehealth/primary-care access prediction
Reinterpret EC commuting as job-housing accessibility via 2SFCA/R2SFCA job-catchment surfaces
Learned gravity/flow generator from 2019-2024 device flows -> demand-aware R2SFCA distance-decay
Link commuter-loss classes to transit/VMT and EV-charging efficiency-vs-equity siting
Physics-informed graph operator over the national commuting graph -> transferable shock simulator
